text.title: Modeling Levels and Database Paradigms
text.summary: Three mandatory modeling levels (conceptual, logical, physical) and six database paradigms — matching paradigm to access pattern and data structure is the primary data modeling decision.

rule.statement: Always progress through all three levels: conceptual (entities and relationships in business terms), logical (normalized attributes, keys, cardinality — technology-agnostic), physical (tables, data types, indexes for a specific engine). Skipping logical produces unmaintainable physical schemas.
rule.rationale: Conceptual validates business understanding with stakeholders. Logical catches normalization issues before they become expensive to fix in production DDL. Physical translates to the target engine's capabilities.

list.item: Conceptual: entity names only, high-level relationships, no attributes, stakeholder communication
Logical: attributes, primary/foreign keys, 1:1/1:N/M:N cardinality, normalization to 3NF, technology-agnostic
Physical: table/column names, exact data types, indexes, partitioning, engine-specific constraints

rule.statement: Match the database paradigm to the dominant access pattern and data structure. Do not default to relational for every problem.
rule.rationale: A relational schema running frequent time-range aggregations on 10B rows is slower than a time-series hypertable by orders of magnitude. Paradigm mismatch is the most common source of irreversible performance failures.

list.item: Relational (PostgreSQL, MySQL): structured data, complex relationships, ACID required, OLTP — use for orders, users, financial records
Document (MongoDB, DynamoDB): flexible schema, hierarchical data, read-heavy catalog — use for product catalogs, user profiles, content
Key-Value (Redis, DynamoDB): extreme performance, simple lookups — use for sessions, caches, leaderboards
Wide-Column (Cassandra, ScyllaDB): high write throughput, time-series, horizontal scale — use for IoT, activity feeds, audit logs
Graph (Neo4j, Neptune): highly connected data, relationship traversal — use for social networks, fraud detection, recommendations
Time-Series (TimescaleDB, InfluxDB): timestamped data points, aggregation, retention — use for metrics, sensors, financial ticks

rule.statement: Use multiple database paradigms within one system when different data domains have fundamentally different access patterns. Define clear ownership boundaries and synchronization strategy per domain.
rule.rationale: No single database is optimal for OLTP transactions, full-text search, time-series metrics, and graph traversal simultaneously. Polyglot persistence assigns each concern to its optimal store, at the cost of operational complexity and synchronization logic.

example.code: PostgreSQL → orders, users, payments (ACID transactions) Redis → sessions, rate limits, cache (sub-millisecond) Elasticsearch → product search, full-text queries Neo4j → product recommendations, customer graph ClickHouse → analytics, reporting aggregations
